claim.query: protestant presbyterian minority church of ireland mixed civic labour

claim.thesis: Measure the decline of confessional and civic plurality inside the press slice of the archive.

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XII NO. 3, MARCH 10 1906
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
North charged with the special mission of reporting the

broceedings at the election then pending in the isolated

portion of England known as Ulster, and I felt no

special pleasure in the task. Things had not gone well

with the North. I recollect excellently the intense in-

terest with which Mr. Bryce’s unique experiment in

egislation had been received by students of European

politics in 1907.  The separation of Norway from

Sweden, the disintegration of the Austrian Empire and

the other political surprises which marked the years 1905

and.1906, had caused much excitement in England. The

Liberal Party returned to power by an exceedingly

arge majority, had found themselves confronted by a

United Irish and Labour Party that would not brook

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XII NO. 7, APRIL 7 1906
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
Papal Legate in hot haste to make an American Bishop

behave himself ; the disregard of Dr. O’Dwyer, and of all

ecclesiastics, in fact, for the secular education of Catho-

lics; and so on, and so.on, through acres of letter-writ-

ing you can track the trail of his ink, which should

have been spared for the single question as to the educa-

. tional rights of Irish Cetholics in England.

Before closing accounts with him I wish to make some

remarks.

' THE- LEADER.

107

1. In all that he has written about the present Edu-

cation question in England, he has shown the most de-

plorable ignorance of it—or if he chooses the other alter-

native, let him. One thing is certain, the statements

he has made on it are, to speak mildly, extravagantly

inaccurate.

[page 10]
Perhaps racially the Celt is more emotional than tho

Ten}ton ; but for all that we believe that there were his-

torical causes behind that richness of emotion, that ful-

ness of heart, that gentlenoss of heart, not only per-

ceived, but dreamt on with love by everyono whom the

Gaelic League has enabled to gaze into the life that

existed in old days in Irish Ireland behind its penal

fastnesses. Yet for the thistorians that have been, not,

thank God, for those that axe, this people, our kin, were

as a people that dwelt in a mist. Only now can we form

somo idea of the big things in their lives: they had a

life apart from that of the atrophied Irish mnation;

worldly riches and kindred thoughts were not for them ;

an “eastern world’’ existed in their poems, and to them

contradictory_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XIII NO. 3, SEPTEMBER 8 1906
contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
order that “ in-consideration thereof ”’ the Governors of

the Hospital should receive 20 boys from the Erasmus

Smith Board to be supported, clothed, educated ,and

pub out as apprentices at the expense of the said Board.

Just think of, say, the President of Rockwell College,

demanding £300 as a consideration for receiving for

ever in his College 20 students, whose pensions should

be paid, whose clothing should be supplied, and who

should get Exhibitions from the Royal University. Yet

such exactly is the “ Agreement '’ entered into between

the Erasmus Smith Board, and the Board of Blue Coat

Hospital.  Such is the “ Agreement '’ that has been

thought by pious people good enough for robbing “Idola-

tors ’’ for 187 years.

The low tricks by which an attempt has been made

contradictory_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XII NO. 6, MARCH 31 1906
contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
makes a gentleman’s linen look as if the rats had been

gnawing the edges of it, and as if it had last been laun-

dered with a soap of the ““ won’t wash clothes”’ variety.

Again, look at his portrait of Mr. Harrington, M.P., in

his robes as Lord Mayor of Dublin. See how the fur of

the robes is handled, and look at that round, yellow

thing hanging out of the chain of office! What is it?

Verily if Northcote had done his work after this fashion,

Sir Joshua would very soon have sent him to the right-

about. There is a Reynolds in the Mansion House,

Dublin—a portrait of a Lord Lieutenant of Treland,

hanging, I think, in the drawing-room. Let Mr. Yeats

step in and study that picture, and then cross over to

Abbey St., and look at his own! Certainly, I hope that

foreign country the postage is one penny.

“. Strange,”” said the Johnnie, “I cawn’t get this
cigarette anywhere but in Mullingar.”” Oh, Mullingar,
fis on you'! R

The serious nature of the “bona fide” nuisance at
such places as Booterstown was emphasised by a recent
Prosecution of a bogus ““bona’’ who was found on the
Premises of a bungery owner by the name of Johu
Downey. According to evidence there wers 40 persons
in the bungery, and over 100 in the yard attached.
Ov‘er 140 “bonas”’ on the premises of one Booterstown
drink shop on a Sunday! Downey, ‘who runs this
*{h’lnk _shop, and who was fined £5 in 1903 for supply-
ing drink to a drunken man, was fined £2 on the head
of this bogus “bona.” It is not without a smile that
one reads that the name of the man on guard at the
door is John Plant, and that the policeman who bagged
the bogus “bona’’ is by name Sergeant Beery! Send
a Beery to catch a “bona!’”’

A great outburst of Orange raimeis took place onc
day near Bushmills, where, appropriately enough,
whiskey is made. The resolution proposed by Captain
Craig, M.P., bore out one of our contentions—namély,
that “ The King ”’ is a party cry in Ireland. T

contextual_chunks.title: The Leader, vol. XIII no. 8, October 13 1906
contextual_chunks.text:
for every advertisement four shillings sterling.”’ Four
shillings for about three-quarters of a Freeman column!
And Valentin and Co. expect an editorial note—sup-
plied by themselves—concerning their ‘ well-known
house *’ given in as a “tilley!”” And there are papers
in Ireland that take Hamburg lottery advertisements,
whether of this particular firm or not we do not know.
Henceforward if our readers.notice a Hamburg lottery
advertisement in their local papers, let them bear in
mind that Valentin and Co. “are disposed to pay for
every advertisement four shillings sterling,”’ and expect
an editorial puff about their “ well-known house.”” Oh,
what a Press!

One day last month Miss Eileen Murphy, daughter
of Dr. J. J. Murphy, of Harcourt Street, Dublin, re-
ceived the white veil in the historic convent of the Irish
Benedictine Nuns at Ypres, where the flag of the Irish
Brigade and flags of Ramillies were deposited. In those
Irish-Treland days one naturally foels that if the “ Wild
Geese’’ had fought in Ireland instead of at Ramillies
and other places, it would have been better for the
country, and likewise many of us regret the emigration
of nuns as we do of oth
